quiz.title: Geometry: Composite Solids — Volume
quiz.subtitle: Unit 11 Quiz 9: Calculating Volume of Combined or Hollowed Solids (10 Questions)
quiz.subject: Geometry

questions.question: What is the general strategy for finding the volume of a composite solid?
options: Add volumes of component solids (combined shapes) or subtract removed volumes (hollowed shapes)
Find the surface area first, then divide by the height
Average the volumes of the individual solids
Multiply the base perimeter by the total height
questions.explanation: Volume of composite solids is straightforward: for combined shapes, add the volumes of the components; for shapes with holes or carved-out regions, subtract the volume of the removed part. Unlike surface area, there are no hidden faces to worry about — volume is simply the total space occupied.

questions.question: A square pyramid sits exactly on top of a rectangular prism. The prism has base $8 \times 8$ ft and height $10$ ft. The pyramid has the same base and height $6$ ft. What is the total volume?
questions.explanation: Prism: $V = 8 \times 8 \times 10 = 640$ ft$^3$. Pyramid: $V = \frac{1}{3} \times 8^2 \times 6 = \frac{1}{3} \times 384 = 128$ ft$^3$. Total $= 640 + 128 = 768$ ft$^3$. No hidden volumes to subtract — just add the two components.

questions.question: A cone sits on top of a cylinder. Both have radius $5$ cm. The cylinder height is $12$ cm and the cone height is $9$ cm. What is the total volume in terms of $\pi$?
questions.explanation: Cylinder: $V = \pi(5^2)(12) = 300\pi$ cm$^3$. Cone: $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi(5^2)(9) = 75\pi$ cm$^3$. Total $= 300\pi + 75\pi = 375\pi$ cm$^3$. The $\frac{1}{3}$ factor makes the cone exactly one-quarter of the total volume.

questions.question: A hemisphere sits on top of a cylinder. Both have radius $4$ m. The cylinder height is $10$ m. What is the total volume, rounded to the nearest tenth?
A cylinder of radius $6$ in and height $20$ in has a cylindrical hole of radius $2$ in drilled completely through its center along the axis. What is the remaining volume in terms of $\pi$?
questions.explanation: Outer cylinder: $V = \pi(6^2)(20) = 720\pi$ in$^3$. Hole: $V = \pi(2^2)(20) = 80\pi$ in$^3$. Remaining $= 720\pi - 80\pi = 640\pi$ in$^3$. The hole removes $\frac{1}{9}$ of the original volume (since the hole radius is $\frac{1}{3}$ of the outer radius, area ratio is $\frac{1}{9}$).

questions.question: A hemisphere of radius $3$ cm is carved out of one face of a cube of side $8$ cm. What is the remaining volume, rounded to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation: Cube: $V = 8^3 = 512$ cm$^3$. Hemisphere removed: $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi(3^3) = \frac{2}{3}\pi(27) = 18\pi \approx 56.5$ cm$^3$. Remaining $= 512 - 56.5 = 455.5$ cm$^3$. The carved region removes about $11\%$ of the cube.

questions.question: A grain silo consists of a cylinder of diameter $10$ ft and height $15$ ft, topped by a hemisphere. What is the total volume, rounded to the nearest cubic foot?
questions.explanation: Radius $= 5$ ft. Cylinder: $V = \pi(5^2)(15) = 375\pi$ ft$^3$. Hemisphere: $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi(5^3) = \frac{250\pi}{3}$ ft$^3$. Total $= 375\pi + \frac{250\pi}{3} = \frac{1375\pi}{3} \approx 1{,}440.0 \approx 1{,}440$ ft$^3$.

questions.question: A hemisphere is carved out of the base of a cone. Both have radius $6$ cm, and the cone height is $14$ cm. What is the remaining volume, rounded to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation: Cone: $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi(6^2)(14) = 168\pi$ cm$^3$. Hemisphere removed: $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi(6^3) = 144\pi$ cm$^3$. Remaining $= 168\pi - 144\pi = 24\pi \approx 75.4$ cm$^3$. Most of the cone's volume is removed — only $\frac{1}{7}$ remains.

questions.question: A capsule-shaped water tank consists of a cylinder with a hemisphere at each end. The cylinder has radius $3$ ft and length $10$ ft. What is the total volume, rounded to the nearest cubic foot?
questions.explanation: Cylinder: $V = \pi(3^2)(10) = 90\pi$ ft$^3$. Two hemispheres form a full sphere: $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi(3^3) = 36\pi$ ft$^3$. Total $= 90\pi + 36\pi = 126\pi \approx 395.8 \approx 396$ ft$^3$. A capsule is just a cylinder plus a sphere.

questions.question: A cylindrical rod of radius $2$ cm and length $15$ cm has one end sharpened into a cone of height $3$ cm (the cone replaces the original cylinder tip). What is the volume of material removed, in terms of $\pi$?
questions.explanation: Original cylinder tip: $V = \pi(2^2)(3) = 12\pi$ cm$^3$. Cone tip remaining: $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi(2^2)(3) = 4\pi$ cm$^3$. Material removed $= 12\pi - 4\pi = 8\pi$ cm$^3$. Exactly $\frac{2}{3}$ of the original tip is removed — that's the difference between a cylinder and a cone of the same base and height.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered volume of composite solids. You correctly add volumes of combined shapes, subtract for hollowed regions, and handle all combinations — prisms, cylinders, cones, pyramids, spheres, and hemispheres.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Remember: (1) For combined solids, add the component volumes, (2) For hollowed solids, subtract the removed volume, (3) Two hemispheres form a full sphere ($\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$), (4) A cylinder minus a cone of same base and height removes $\frac{2}{3}$ of the cylinder's volume.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: (1) identifying each component solid correctly, (2) using the right volume formula for each ($\frac{1}{3}$ for cones/pyramids, $\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ for spheres), (3) for drilled holes, the hole is a cylinder, (4) for carved hemispheres, use $\frac{2}{3}\pi r^3$ for the removed volume.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! For combined solids, add volumes: $V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2 + ...$ For hollowed solids, subtract: $V_{\text{remaining}} = V_{\text{original}} - V_{\text{removed}}$. Volume is simpler than surface area because there are no hidden faces. Just identify the shapes and apply the correct formulas.
